coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots modifying sphere-flower CdIn2S4 on N-rGO sheet muti-dimensional photocatalyst for efficient visible degradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chongyang
Li, Xin
Li, Jinze
Sun, Linlin
Zhou, Yaju
Guan, Jingru
Wang, Huiqin
Huo, Pengwei
Ma, Changchang
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: The muti-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modifying sphere-flower CdIn2S4 on nitrogen doping on reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO) photocatalyst is fabricated through the simple hydrothermal method and solvothermal method. CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO composites show good photocatalytic degradation for 80% 50 mg/L 2,4-dichlorophenol under visible light for 6 h, which could be ascribable to the good conductive property and defects of N-rGO sheet (two dimension) and the heterojunction constructed between CQDs (zero dimension) and sphere-flower CdIn2S4 (three dimension), and synergism among them. These are evidenced by the photoluminescence spectra, photocurrent intensity, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and Raman spectra. Besides, the results of ESR show that ·O2 − and ·OH radicals both are generated under visible light, ·OH the main one further confirmed by free radicals trapping experiments. This work will give some inspirations for constructing multi-dimensional semiconductors with excellent photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
N-rGO
CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO
Visible light
Photodegradate 2,4-dichlorophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-rGO, sphere-flower CdIn2S4 and CQDs
The effects of pH and concentration of photocatalyst
Active species trapping and quantification experiments

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of (a) GO, rGO and N-rGO, (b) (A) CdIn2S4, (B) CdIn2S4/N-rGO (1 wt%), (C) CdIn2S4/N-rGO (3 wt%), (D) CdIn2S4/N-rGO (5 wt%), (E) CdIn2S4/N-rGO (7 wt%), (F) CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO (0.3 mL 5 wt%), (G) CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO (0.5 mL 5 wt%), (H) CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO (0.7 mL 5 wt%), (I) CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO (1.0 mL 5 wt%).
SEM images of (A) N-rGO, (B) the EDS image of N-rGO, (C) and (D) CdIn2S4, (E) and (F) CdIn2S4/N-rGO, (G) and (H) CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO.
Raman spectra of samples we have prepared.
UV–vis DRS of (A) the samples, and (B) the plots of transformed Kubelka–Munk function vs hv spectra of the CdIn2S4.
The Nitrogen absorption–desorption isotherm of samples.
(A) Transient photocurrent response and (B) EIS spectra of samples.
Photodegrade 2,4-DCP under visible-light irradiation for certain time and first-kinetic curves (C) and recycling experiment (D).
Trapping experiment for photodegrading 2,4-DCP over CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalyst (EDTA for h+, isopropanol for ·OH, ascorbic acid for ·O2 −) under visible light irradiation.
ESR signals of CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO composite with light on or not. (A) DMPO-·OH; (B) DMPO-·O2 −.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots modifying sphere-flower CdIn2S4 on N-rGO sheet muti-dimensional photocatalyst for efficient visible degradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The muti-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modifying sphere-flower CdIn2S4 on nitrogen doping on reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO) photocatalyst is fabricated through the simple hydrothermal method and solvothermal method. CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO composites show good photocatalytic degradation for 80% 50 mg/L 2,4-dichlorophenol under visible light for 6 h, which could be ascribable to the good conductive property and defects of N-rGO sheet (two dimension) and the heterojunction constructed between CQDs (zero dimension) and sphere-flower CdIn2S4 (three dimension), and synergism among them. These are evidenced by the photoluminescence spectra, photocurrent intensity, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and Raman spectra. Besides, the results of ESR show that ·O2 − and ·OH radicals both are generated under visible light, ·OH the main one further confirmed by free radicals trapping experiments. This work will give some inspirations for constructing multi-dimensional semiconductors with excellent photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenol and phenolic compounds (like chlorophenols (CPs), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP)), mainly originating from chemical plants, coal industry, oil refineries, and their like, are wildly employed in may aspects in our daily life [1]. However, they are toxic pollutants hard to decompose for their stable structure that they mainly contain chlorine atoms and the ring of benzene [2]. Besides, they are easy to biological accumulation from circulatory of the ecosystem, denaturing protein of organism, even causing death [3–5]. 2,4-DCP, on behalf of the products for characteristic herbicide, are utilized in the filed of agriculture and manufacturing plants [6,7]. At the same time, the higher concentration of herbicide is infiltrating the ground water, and pouring into the river, further resulting in wastewater [8,9]. As a consequence, it is necessary to eliminate CPs from wastewater. Some methods has been springing up to solve this problem, such as liquid phase micro-extraction [10,11], electrochemical treatments [12,13], adsorption [14,15] and et al. However, these methods assume more energy and some more organic solvents. While the photocatalysis, an alternative and promising technique, could degrade organic pollutants, and capture people's attention in recent years [16–18], especially the photocatalyst could degrade CPs from waster under the sunlight irradiation, like C/ZnO/CdS nanocomposite [19], Ag/CDots/BiOBr ternary photocatalyst [20], 2D porous graphitic C3N4 nanosheets/Ag3PO4 [21].

Among the semiconductor photocatalyst, metal sulfides for their suitable band gap and good coefficient have caught people attention [22–24]. Binary metal sulfides (CdS, CuS) are susceptible to photocorression in the progress that photogenerated holes created by electrons of valence band excited transfer to conductive band have impact on them. There are several methods to handle it, such as constructing heterojunction, loading on supporting materials, adulterating with other compounds, mainly by incorporation into their interlayers. However, that incorporating steps could lead to poor photocatalytic performance [25]. Compared with binary metal sulfides, ternary ones with strong visible light absorption and narrow forbidden band are becoming an focus. CdIn2S4, as a member of family that belong to AB2C4 ternary compounds, has a direct band gap (2.0–2.2 eV) [26], exhibiting a good photocatalytic activity under the visible light. Although CdIn2S4 nanomaterials are employed in many aspects, like lighting-emitting diodes, photocatalyst, solar cells, photoelectrical application and so on [27–29], the further application is still confined by high recombination of photocarries. Therefore, several methods have been springing up to handle it. One way is that change the morphology of CdIn2S4 to tune the band gap. Huang et al. made it that CdIn2S4 microsphere could effectively degrade the Azo Dye [30]. Another way is that construct heterostructure to reduce the recombination rate of photocarries. It is reported that Sun et al. had been successful in synthesizing hierarchical ZnIn2S4/CdIn2S4 microspheres that they could degrade 99.7% organic dyes after 90 min [31]. The other way is loading on supporting materials. Xiao et al. prepared RGO/CdIn2S4/g-C3N4 ternary hybrid photocatalysts that they could show significantly efficient photocatalytic activities (10 mg/L tetracycline hydrochloride degradation rate up to 74% for 3 h) [32].

Among numerous photocatalyst, carbon materials have drawn people's attention for their excellent conductivity, good physicochemical properties, great absorption ability and et al. Therefore, it is significant to design heterostructure to reduce the recombination of photocarries and enlarge the visible light absorption range. The zero-dimensional CQDs with prominent morphology of amorphous or turbostratic carbon, as an emerging novel nano-carbon material, are composed of zero-dimensional structures within typical 10 nm size in diameter [33]. CQDs get more studies due to its applications in bioimaging for low toxicity and good biocompatibility [34], optics for outstanding electrochemical properties [35], selective oxidation for excellent PL and photochemical abilities [36], and its like. It is noticeable that CQDs have the up-converted capability [37], thus making full use of sunlight spectrum ascribed to the conjugated π-domains [38], and PL emission associated with surface state, conjugated structures, and special structure sites [39], which contribute to improvement of photocatalytic performance. There are many experiments that the introduction of CQDs into other photocatalyst could effectively improve the photocatalytic ability, which are ascribed to photoinduced electron-accepting properties [40]. What iss more, CQDs could convert long wavelength into short wavelength [41,42]. It is effective that CQDs could combine themselves with other composites, thus constructing heterostructure, which is beneficial to improve the whole photocatalytic performance for these CQDs could function as photoinduced electron transfer and acceptor [43]. For example, Pan et al. synthesized that Z-scheme CdS/CQDs/BiOCl heterojunction could efficiently decompose phenol that the 42% 10 ppm phenol was photodegraded under UV light irradiation for 2 h [44]. Liang et al. made it that carbon quantum dots modifying BiOBr microspheres could degrade 94.3% rhodamine under visible irradiation for 30 min [45]. Li et al. could successfully prepare Bi/BiOCl-TiO2 CQDs quaternary photocatalyst by which about 94% of MO and 82% of PNP were degraded under visible-light irradiation for 100 min and 240 min, respectively [46].

It is known that recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and electrons transfer are two key factors that estimate photocatalytic performance. Another carbon material is graphene, a two-dimensional nano-sheet material. In order to overcome the shortcomings (like zero band gap, weak interlayer force) of graphene, adulterating other heteroatoms (such as N, P, Cl) into carbon lattice of graphene could effectively tailor itself, thus bringing in physicochemical properties. Nitrogen atom, comparable atomic size as carbon atoms, contributes to forming strong bonds [47,48]. There are many reports that show that N-rGO could change the spin density and charge distribution, thus creating active regions and influencing the lattice alignment, so as to improve the photocatalytic activity [49–51]. What is more, synergistic function between two components takes place. In addition, N-rGO could function as support material, which is beneficial to the dispersion of semiconductors [52,53]. In recent years, that combine N-rGO with other semiconductors has gained extensive attention, like Co3O4/N-RGO [54], Pd50Ag50/Fe3O4/N-rGO [55], Cu–Zn/N-rGO [56], and et al.

In this paper, zero dimensional CQDs modifying three-dimensional sphere-flower CdIn2S4 loading on amorphous two dimensional N-rGO nano-sheet composite photocatalyst was successfully prepared via a facile hydrothermal method and solvothermal method. We construct the semiconductor heterojunction between CQDs and CdIn2S4, further changing the path of photogenerated electron and hole pairs and prolonging the lifetime of carries. N-rGO could not only benefit the electron transfer, but also it gives more active sites, further improving photodegradation of 2,4-DCP. And the mechanism of CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO is studied through free radicals trapping experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-rGO, sphere-flower CdIn2S4 and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous research [57], the preparation of N-rGO was listed in supporting information. The sphere-flower CdIn2S4 photocatalysts were got by the hydrothermal method. Thiourea (4 mmol), cadmium chloride (1 mmol) and Indium(III) chloride tetrahydrate (2 mmol) were added into 30 mL deionized water under ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h, and then transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave. Then the samples were washed by deionized water for several times before heated at 180 °C for 48 h, and parched at 60 °C; 1.6 g ascorbic acid, 15 mL ethylene glycol and 15 mL deionized water were put into Teflon-lined autoclave, and then kept at 160 °C in the oven for 2 h after undergoing ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h. We obtained CQDs though dialysis.

The different mass fraction of N-rGO were added into the synthesis of CdIn2S4. We obtained a series of CdIn2S4/N-rGO photoctalysts. The better ration of CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalysts were added into Teflon-lined autoclave with 20 mL deionized water. The different volume of CQDs were added into it. Then mixture were heated at 140 °C in the oven for 5 h after ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h. The samples were washed with deionized water, and then were dried at 60 °C (Scheme 1 ).

The crystal structure of the samples was collected by the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD model MAC Science, Japan, Ni-filtrated Cu-Kα radiation) for the scan rate of 5°/min. The elements of the surface for samples were tested by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PerkinElmer PHI 5300, Mg Kα source) and the C 1s signal was set to 284.6 eV. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were shown through a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7001F, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2010, Japan), respectively. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was done on a FEI Tecnai G2F20 instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the catalysts were performed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450; Shimazu, Japan). While the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were performed by an F-4500 (Hitachi, Japan) photoluminescence detector. And Raman spectra of the samples are done on Raman equipment (a 532 nm laser DXR spectrometer.)

The photocurrent experiments were done on a electrochemical system (CHI 660B, Shanghai, China) equipped with a standard three-electrode quartz cells that a Ag/AgCl electrode and a Pt wire were served as the reference electrode and counter electrode, the samples the working electrode [58,59], the 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was implemented with amplitude of applied sine 10 mV in a 0.5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− and KCl solution with a frequency ranging from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. The EIS tests also were done on the same system.

The formation of active species (·O2 − and ·OH radicals) were tested by ESR (Brucker A300 ESR spectrometer) at room temperature; and the ESR signals of spin-trapped paramagnetic species were confirmed by 5,5-diamethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) [58]. The ·OH radicals were detected in ultrapure water, ·O2 − radicals in methanol. The ESR spectra were measured under a 450 W xenon lamp to simulate the solar irradiation for several minutes.

In a bid to investigate the crystallographic structures of the samples we obtained, the XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 1 . It is apparent that the characteristic sharp peak of GO is at 10.8°, which has an interlayer spacing of 0.7 nm, bettering the hydration and exfoliation of layers and layers. When the hydrated hydrazine is added, as a reducing agent, the full width at half maxima, the peak at around 24.5 °corresponding to 002 plane of graphite, becomes larger rather than sharp peak, because of removal of oxygen atoms inserting into graphite gallery [60]. It is worthwhile to note that the patterns of N-rGO similar to these of rGO show that N-rGO could not change the structure for further when the nitrogen atoms are doped on graphene. What's more, the peak at 43°, corresponding to 100 reflection, is associated with sp3 carbon atoms between CO bonding for the change of structure in the graphene. Obviously, the order of relative intensity of 100 peaks of the samples we obtained is N-rGO>rGO>GO. That is to say, sp3 carbon atoms bonding and restorations of the in-plane is closely related with 100 peak. The more strong intensity is, the more contents of oxygen is [61]. Noticeable, the full width at half maxima of N-rGO is larger than rGO and GO, which suggests that the layer to layer of N-rGO could become larger, bettering increase of specific area and active sites. In Fig. 1(b), the main characteristic peaks of CdIn2S4 are located at 2θ = 14.1°, 23.2°, 27.3°, 28.5°, 33.0°, 40.7°, 43.3° indexed to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (511), (440) planes of cubic spinel CdIn2S4(PDF# 27-0060). There are no other impurity peaks detected such as CdS, In2S3, which indicates that the sample of CdIn2S4 has high purity. What's more, these peaks are sharp and strong, suggesting that good crystalline of CdIn2S4. After the introduction of N-rGO, the diffraction peaks of CdIn2S4 do have no change, which means that the crystalline structure of CdIn2S4 could not have been affected. That is because the mass content of N-rGO is low. It is worthwhile to note that the introduction of CQDs to CdIn2S4/N-rGO composite photoctalyst brings about the downside of peaks for CdIn2S4, which may be ascribed to that CQDs could combine themselves with CdIn2S4.

The chemical states and surface compositions of the samples could effectively be described by XPS technique. Fig. 2 (A) shows that C 1 s in N-rGO could be deconvoluted into CC bond at 284.6 eV, CO bond at 286.31 eV, CN bond at 285.28 eV, C=O bond at 286.88 eV, and OC=O bond at 289.04 eV, and the shakeup at 291.25 eV [62]. When CdIn2S4 photocatalysts load on N-rGO, the position of CO bond shifts upside, which may be due to the interaction between CdIn2S4 and N-rGO. However, the introducing CQDs into CdIn2S4/N-rGO gives rise to the increasing intensity of C=O bond because of CQDs. It is noticeable that the N 1 s in N-rGO could be divided into four binding energies in Fig. 2(B) [62]. The spectrum of N 1s for CdIn2S4/N-rGO changes a lot for the overlap of Cd 3d and N 1 s hardly separated. Apparently, the four peaks of O 1s could are shown in Fig. 2(C). The COOH binding energy of CdIn2S4/N-rGO shifts upside, which may be ascribed to the interaction. While CQDs modify CdIn2S4/N-rGO, the intensity of COOH bonds get strengthened for the CQDs itself. In Fig. 2(D), the distinct peaks of CdIn2S4 and CdIn2S4/N-rGO at 404.78 eV and 411.58 eV are in accordance with Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, suggesting that Cd2+ ions occur in spinel structures, which coincides with XRD results. Whereas the positions of them in CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO shift upside, it may be due to the CQDs modifying CdIn2S4/N-rGO by transferring electrons from CdIn2S4 to CQDs. So does the In element. The S 2p spectrum exhibits S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 at 160.4 eV and 161.5 eV, respectively, indicating that S2− ions appear in three types of photocatalysts. While the peaks located at 162.9 eV and 163.7 eV observed are sulfur elements and compounds. In table S1, atomic per cent of high-resolution scans are listed and applied to evaluate the element ratio. The element rations of Cd, In, S in CdIn2S4 is some 1:2:4, which indirectly confirms that CdIn2S4 photocatalysts have been successfully made. No matter which those in CdIn2S4, CdIn2S4/N-rGO or CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO is about 1:2:4, suggesting that the structure and composition of CdIn2S4 have great stability during the synthesizing process, corresponding to the XRD results.

The morphology and nanocrystallite size of synthesizing samples were shown via the SEM, TEM and HRTEM in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(A) exhibits the smooth surface of two-dimensional N-rGO sheet showed amorphous structure, the EDS image of N-rGO showing the N, C, O elements, implying that the N-rGO has been successfully made, while the several layers of it was about 10 nm in Fig. S1A. The three-dimensional sphere-flower CdIn2S4 material was composed of octahedron, which was beneficial to increase the specific surface area, thus improving photocatalytic ability in Fig. 3(C) and (E). The phenomenon accords with rhombic appearance in Fig. S1B. The formation of sphere-flower CdIn2S4 is because Ostwald ripening takes place, and the larger nanocrystals formed by small ones dissolved contribute to the decrease of surface energy to form the sphere morphology [63]. At the same time, the morphology of CdIn2S4 was consistent with results of XRD. After introduction of N-rGO into CdIn2S4, as shown in Fig. 3(E) and (F), it is noticeable that lots of CdIn2S4 particles are adhered on amorphous N-rGO, and some CdIn2S4 particles get well dispersed, further increasing more active sites to photocatalytic 2,4-DCP, which are confirmed in Fig. S1D. The characteristic form of zero-dimensional CQDs are dots with average size of 4 nm in Fig. S1C, with lattice spacing of 0.326 nm, which is consistent with the (002) lattice planes of graphite [64], meaning that CQDs are in possession of graphite-like structure in Fig. S2D. When zero-dimensional CQDs modified the CdIn2S4/N-rGO, the surface of sphere-flower CdIn2S4 had become smooth comparing with this in Fig. 3(C). Fig. S2 shows that rhombic CdIn2S4 and some grains are closely dispersed on N-rGO sheets. What is more, the lattice spacing of 0.313 nm is consistent with the (222) plane of cubic CdIn2S4, 0.27 nm the (400) plane of that. What is more, the lattices are staggered, which suggests that heterojunction may be constructed.

With aim to study the structure at atom degree of the samples, the Raman measurement is a helpful way by testing the D and G bands that are characteristic feature of carbon materials. In Fig. 4 , the D and G bands at 1369 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1 for N-rGO move downside compare with those of rGO, much more than those of GO for the reason that hydrazine hydrate as strong reduced agents reduces GO to exfoliate films and nitrogen atoms are inserted into the backbone of graphene [65,66]. Moreover, the intensity of D band associated with C sp3 of the hexagonal graphitic structure that decides the defects for N-rGO is stronger than that for rGO, which may be responsible for nitrogen atoms inserted, while that for GO is strongest, for which the oxygen-containing functional groups introduced destroy the graphitic structure [67]. The I D/I G values could gauge effectively the hybridized defects. Distinctly, I D/I G values of GO is smaller than that for rGO, showing that GO is reduced, and that for N-rGO, suggesting that nitrogen atoms introduced create more defects. Fig. 4(B) shows Raman spectra of some samples. In light of the Raman spectra that Γ=A1g+Eg+F1g + 3F 2g + 2A 2u + 2Eu + 4F 1u + 2F 2u , it is noted that four vibrational modes in CdIn2S4 crystalline are A1g at 359 cm−1, F2g at 88 cm−1, F2g at 243 cm−1 and E g at 176 cm−1 [68]. That all peaks shift downside may be ascribed to the grain sides [68]. No matter when introduce the N-rGO or the both N-rGO and CQDs into the CdIn2S4 crystalline, the locations of peaks do not change, which means that CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO composite photocatalyst has been made. In Fig. 4(S), although the some peaks of CdIn2S4 could have influence on D and G bands, the I D/I G is increasing, suggesting that many defects are created, beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic performance.

With the aim of grasping the optical properties and band gaps for samples we have obtained, they are tested by UV-vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) in Fig. 5 . Noticeably, no matter which the rGO or N-rGO is, they all show great response to the visible light, which may be ascribed to the black body. The flower-sphere CdIn2S4 photocatalysts show good absorption edge of 630 nm. After introducing N-rGO, the absorption edge has distinct red shift, suggesting that CdIn2S4 photocatalysts have succeeded in loading on N-rGO, which is consistent with the images of SEM. That may be due to the interaction between N-rGO and CdIn2S4 [69]. Compared with CdIn2S4/N-rGO, the ternary composites show blue shift, which may be caused by the up-converted capability of CQDs and the heterojunction formed [37,42]. On the basis of the formula of band gap: αhν= A(hν-Eg)2, where α, h, ν, A, Eg means the absorption coefficient, the Planck constant, the light frequency, a constant, and the band gap. Fig. 5(B) shows the forbidden band of CdIn2S4 is 1.96 eV, which is smaller than the literature report [30]. This phenomenon could be responsible for the flower-sphere morphology.

To determine which types of factors about specific surface and pore volume to better the photocatalytic process, the BET gas-sorption measurements are done and analysis are shown in Fig. 6 . All the samples show the H3-type hysteresis loop and Langmuir IV behavior [69]. According to the result we collect, the specific surface areas of CdIn2S4, CdIn2S4/N-rGO, CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO are 5.532 m2/g, 2.465 m2/g, 2.136 m2/g. This consequence may be because that some of sphere-flower CdIn2S4 photocatalysts are broken, and some CQDs reunite together, causing the overlap of specific surface areas for CdIn2S4 and N-rGO and CQDs covering the surface of CdIn2S4, which are confirmed by the SEM images. While the pore size of CdIn2S4, CdIn2S4/N-rGO, CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO are 28.20 nm, 53.15 nm, 48.95 nm, respectively, which may be ascribed to dispersion of N-rGO and reunion of CQDs. In summary, the defects and active sites of N-rGO and the up-converted property of CQDs enhance the whole photocatalytic performance.

To effectively evaluate the transfer and recombination of photogenerated carries, PL is regarded as a useful measurement associated with the optical properties. In Fig. 7 , all the samples show strong emission peak when they are under the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. Compared with CdIn2S4, the CdIn2S4/N-rGO have lower intensity of PL, implying that the combination of photogenerated carries gets inhibited by CdIn2S4 loading on N-rGO to obtain CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalyst, which may be owing to the defects of N-rGO trapping the electrons and synergism between CdIn2S4 and N-rGO [70,71]. However, the PL intensity of CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO is stronger than CdIn2S4 and CdIn2S4/N-rGO, which is attributed to the PL properties of CQDs [72]. At the same time, it means that CQDs has made it that it has coupled with CdIn2S4/N-rGO, which is consistent with the images of SEM and HRTEM. And the photogenerated electrons excited from the CdIn2S4 transfer to zero-dimensional CQDs because of the PL intensity of CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO stronger than that of CdIn2S4/N-rGO.

With the aim of figuring out the photogenerated charge carries transfer ability, photo-response of the samples we have obtained, the photoelectrochemical experiments are implemented. The photocurrents of CdIn2S4, CdIn2S4/N-rGO, and CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO all are recorded during three times light off-and-on in Fig. 8 (A). Noticeably, the photocurrent of CdIn2S4/N-rGO is nearly two times than that of Cdin2S4, implying that low efficient of recombination of electrons happens, which may be caused by good conductive properties of N-rGO for its defects and conjugated π structure [73]. When the CQDs modify the CdIn2S4/N-rGO binary composites, the photocurrent gets stronger, and the difference values between the light on and light off is largest. This could may be on this account that the heterojunction is constructed between CQDs and CdIn2S4, further changing the pathway of electrons transfer and promoting the separation of e−/h+, bettering photocatalytic ability. Besides, the EIS experiments are also capable of showing the surface natures and reflecting the interfacial charge carrier dynamics. From Fig. 8(B), the radius of arc for CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO is smaller than this of CdIn2S4/N-rGO, which may be on account of the conjugated π structure and electrons transfer of CQDs and the π-conjugated system extended by CQDs and N-rGO, while that for CdIn2S4/N-rGO is done than CdIn2S4, which could be attributable to the defects of N-rGO trapping the photogenerated electrons and holes and conjugated π structure [74].

For the sake of measuring the photocatalytic activity of synthesizing samples, we select 2,4-DCP as target pollutants to decompose. The variations of concentration for 2,4-DCP are tested by the absorption through UV–vis spectrophotometer at wavelength (284 nm). In the light of the formula
η =
C t
C 0
, where C t and C 0 are on behalf of the concentration of degrading 2,4-DCP for t time long and the initial concentration that the adsorption–desorption balance has acquired under the dark for 0.5 h, respectively, we can evaluate the photocatalytic ability. In Fig. 9 , it is apparent that all the samples have good photocatalyst performance under the visible light. When CdIn2S4 photocatalysts are on N-rGO, the degradation rate gets improved in comparison with pure CdIn2S4 and N-rGO, which indirectly confirms that complex photocatalysts have been made. When the mass fraction of N-rGO is 5%, that is to say, the mess ratio of N-rGO and CdIn2S4 is 1:20, the degradation rate is 69%. While the volume of CQDs is 0.5 mL, the CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalyst could photodegrade 80% 2,4-DCP for 6 h under visible light irradiation. The results reveal that N-rGO could provide more active sites and CQDs could bring in the up-converted effect to improve the efficiency of light source, and the synergism takes place, and the heterojunction constructed between CQDs and CdIn2S4/N-rGO. However, when the volume of CQDs is more than 0.5 mL, the photodegradation rate decreases. The phenomenon could be attributed to excess CQDs that undermine the absorption of CdIn2S4 and reunite together on the surface of them. Therefore, the proper volume of CQDs and the mass fraction of N-rGO both are beneficial to form the heterojunction and synergism among them [71].

In the aim of evaluate photocatalytic degradation ability, the mineralization activity could not be ignored. In Fig. S6, it is clear to see that when CdIn2S4 photocatalyst loads on N-rGO, the TOC remove rate could reach 58.26%, indicating that N-rGO could effectively transport electrons to change the transfer path way of electrons, further reducing the recombination of e−\h+ pairs. While CQDs modify CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalyst, 65.52% TOC of 2,4-DCP were removed. In summary, CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalyst shows great photocatalytic performance in comparison with CdIn2S4 and CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalysts.

The recyclability is of significance to evaluate the photocatalyst for practical application. The CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO show good photocatalytic ability to decompose the 2,4-DCP under visible light. Though the degradation rate decreased to 64% after four times recycling, it also has good photocatalytic performance for the structure of some CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalysts destroyed when we collect in Fig. 9(D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of pH and concentration of photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. S7A, with the increasing amount of photocatalyst, the photocatalytic activity get improved, which may be ascribed to the surface area of photocatalyst contacting with the 2,4-DCP molecules. It is noticed that the photocatalytic performance gets inhibited because acid atmosphere that we adjust pH by 2 M HCl lowers the 2,4-DCP ionizing and has positive charge surface on photocatalyst resulting in fewer hydroxyl to form ·OH radicals [75]. However, in Fig. S7B, when we adjust pH with 3 M KOH, the alkaline atmosphere could provide more OH− to make the surface of photocatalyst negative charge, further leading to passive film [76]. This could produce electrostatic repulsion, resulting in adsorption effect of N-rGO. As a result, it could decrease the photocatalytic activity.

In order to figure out the intermediates during the photodegradation of 2,4-DCP, we do the Mass spectrometry (MS) by detecting the anion. According to the Fig. S8., the intermediates are 2-chlorohydroquinone (m/z = 144.5), 4-chlorophenol (m/z = 128.5), 2-chlorophenol (m/z = 128.5), benzene-1,2,4,5-tetrol (m/z = 141), benzoquinone (m/z = 108), butanedioic acid (m/z = 115), hydroquinone (m/z = 110), pyrocatechol (m/z = 110), acrylicacid (m/z = 98), which have been identified. As shown in Fig. S9., once under visible light irradiation, the ·OH radicals produced by photocatalyst attack 2,4-DCP, forming 4-chlorophenol, 2-chlorophenol and 2-chlorohydroquinone by releasing Cl. They are further attacked by ·OH radicals into hydroquinone, pyrocatechol, and benzene-1,2,4,5-tetrol, respectively [57]. With the increasing time of photodegradation, they get attacked by ·O2 − radicals, becoming benzoquinone. Thanks to the ·OH and ·O2 − radicals affecting, benzoquinone molecules become butanedioic acid by the open of ring of benzene. As a series of radicals take part in photocatalytic process, they change into acrylicacid and other small molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species trapping and quantification experiments
ce:section.ce:para: During the photocatalytic process, the types and number of active species greatly influence the efficiency of photodegradation for pollutants. As a consequence, we take a set of trapping scavengers into the photocatalytic process to figure out the active species in Fig. 10 . The best degradation rate is 80%. However, the introducing EDTA and isopropanol respectively into the degradation system bring the degradation rate decrease, especially the ascorbic acid do the worse, suggesting that although all the active radical species take part in photodegradation, the ·O2 − species are main factor because the degradation rate gets greatly curbed (degradation rate of 4%). We can generalize from the results above. No matter which ·OH, ·O2 −, h+ active species all participate actively in photocatalytic process, the influence order of them is ·O2 − > ·OH > h+.

In a bid to investigate the radicals generated in the photocatalytic degradation progress and confirm the mechanism, the ESR technique was done on CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO by DMPO. As is shown in Fig. 11 (A), obviously, under the dark little signals are. When light is on, the series of characteristic peaks of DMPO−·OH adducts with intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 show, implying that the ·OH radicals were produced in the photocatalytic processes [62]. Compared with the signals under the dark, a quartet of peaks of DMPO−·O2 − adduct had shown, suggesting that ·O2 − radicals were generated during the photocatalytic reaction processes. Therefore, the CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalyst we prepared was easily excited under the visible irradiation to produced electrons or holes to react with H2O/O2 molecules, thus producing ·OH and ·O2 − radicals.

Based on the previous result analysis, we bring up the reasonable mechanism of photodegrading 2,4-DCP to give an explanation for enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO composite photocatalyst in Fig. 12 . According to the DRS, XPS valence and Mott−Schottky plots in Fig. 4(S), the band gap of CdIn2S4 is 1.96 eV, and the valence band of it is 0.99 eV that is smaller than E θ (·OH/OH−), the conductive band of it −0.97 eV that is more negative than E θ(O2/·O2 −), which gives rise to O2 reduced into ·O2 − instead of that OH− oxidized into ·OH by CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO photocatalyst irradiated by visible light during the photodegradation system.

Once visible light irradiation, the electrons excited from valence band (VB) transfer to conductive band (CB), further forming the electron-hole pairs. The intensity of electrons exited could accumulate on the surface of CdIn2S4 photocatalyst, and some of them may transfer to the zero-dimensional CQDs, prolonging the lifetime of photogenerated carries, and some may combine with oxygen molecules (O2) to form ·O2 −. The electrons on the surface of CQDs could transfer to two-dimensional N-rGO sheet, then are trapped by the defects of N-rGO. While ·O2 − species could react with H+ (some from the ionization of water and 2,4-DCP) to become H2O2, further combining with the electrons to produce hydroxyl radicals. Meanwhile, 2.4-DCP as pollutants could be photodegraded by the ·O2 − and ·OH. Meantime, the h+ from the photocatalyst could take part in the oxidation species of 2,4-DCP. What's more, the defects and good conductive property of N-rGO could cut down on the recombination of photogenerated charge carries by capturing them and accelerate the velocity of electrons through π–π graphitic carbon network extended by CQDs. Besides, it could photogenerate e−\h+ pairs for its small gap, which is in consistence with DRS results.

In summary, the zero-dimensional CQDs modifying three-dimensional sphere-flower CdIn2S4 on two-dimensional N-rGO sheet composites are prepared by means of simple hydrothermal method and solvothermal method. When the mass fraction of N-rGO is 5% and the volume of CQDs is 0.5 mL, the best photocatalytic performance of CQDs/CdIn2S4/N-rGO on photodegrading 2,4-DCP (50 mg/L) could reach 80%. There improvement could be attributed to the defects of N-rGO and its good conductive properties and unique structure, the separation and excellent charge transfer capabilities of the CQDs, and the synergistic effect among them. It is noted that the constructing muti-dimensional photocatalysts for removing the industrial waste water gives us a new approach to apply to practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21576125 and 21776117), China Postdoctoral Science Founsation (2017M611716 and 2017M611734), Six talent peaks project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. 2016-XCL-014), Zhenjiang Science & Technology Program (Grant No. SH2016012).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2019.03.015.
